metastases were more common in the active monitoring group (9.4% vs. 
~5% in the surgery and RT groups), primarily driven by the patients with 
intermediate-risk disease who were included in the study. By 15-years, 
approximately 70% of patients in the active monitoring arm had received 
treatment.
An updated analysis from the ProtecT trial of 712 patients that had 
pathology available for re-review was reported.264 The authors 
demonstrated that 13.1% of patients had cribriform-positive disease, which 
significantly increased the risk of developing metastases (HR, 3.61; 95% 
CI, 1.60â8.11). RT with neoadjuvant ADT significantly reduced metastasis 
risk (HR, 0.35; 95% CI, 0.16â0.78; Pâ=â.04), whereas surgery did not 
significantly improve long-term outcomes compared with active monitoring 
(15-year cumulative incidence in patients with cribriform-positive disease: 
26% for surgery, 25% for active monitoring, and 8% for RT plus ADT).
Patient-reported outcomes were compared among the 3 groups of 
ProtecT.25,265 The surgery group experienced the greatest negative effect 
on sexual function and urinary continence, whereas the RT group had a 
slight decrease in bowel function. It has been noted that the RT utilized in 
the trial was 3D radiotherapy without image guidance or use of hydrogel 
spacers.266 In addition, studies have shown that active surveillance does 
not adversely impact psychological well-being or QOL.25,267-271
Several large active surveillance cohort studies have shown that >50% of 
those eligible for active surveillance may safely avoid treatment, and thus 
the possible associated side effects of treatment, for at least 10 years (see 
Table 1 below).] For example, in one study, 55% of the population 
remained untreated at 15 years.272 Although a proportion of patients on 
active surveillance will eventually undergo treatment, the delay does not 
appear to impact cure rates, and numerous studies have shown that active 
surveillance can be a safe option for many patients.272-282 In fact, a 2015 
meta-analysis of 26 active surveillance cohort studies that included 7627
patients identified only 8 prostate cancer deaths and 5 cases of 
metastasis.283
For low-risk prostate cancer, active surveillance is preferred.
The favorable intermediate risk grouping was not developed to identify 
optimal active surveillance, but rather to identify patients with intermediate-
risk disease who can safely receive RT alone without ADT. The favorable-
intermediate-risk group includes patients that are well suited for active 
surveillance, such as those with Grade group 1 and PSA <10 ng/mL, with 
a low PSA density. However, it also includes patients with multiple cores 
of Grade group 2 disease with cribriform features and a high PSA density. 
Patients should be counseled that unselected Grade group 2 intermediate-
risk prostate cancer has been shown to have unacceptably high risks of 
metastasis when managed by observation, active monitoring, or active 
surveillance.284 Patients with low-volume disease (ie, 1â2 positive cores), 
low percent or absolute Gleason pattern 4, lack of cribriform features, and 
low PSA density are likely better candidates for active surveillance among 
patients with Grade group 2 disease.264
Therefore, for select patients with favorable intermediate-risk disease, a 
recommendation for active surveillance must be based on careful 
individualized weighing of several factors: life expectancy, general health 
condition, disease characteristics, potential side effects of treatment, and 
patient preference. Shared decision-making, after appropriate counseling 
on the risks and benefits of the various options, is critical. Data in these 
settings are discussed under Patient Selection, below.
The Panel believes there is an urgent need for further clinical research 
regarding the criteria for recommending active surveillance, the criteria for 
reclassification on active surveillance, and the schedule for active 
surveillance especially as it pertains to prostate biopsies, which pose an 
increasing burden.

MS-20
Patient Selection
Oncology Guidelines risk groups are the main determinant used to select patients who 
can safely defer treatment.
Active Surveillance in Low-Risk Disease 
Panel consensus is that active surveillance is preferred for most patients 
with low-risk prostate cancer and a life expectancy â„10 years based on the 
data discussed above. However, the Panel recognizes that there is 
heterogeneity across the low-risk group, and that some factors may be 
associated with an increased probability of near-term grade 
reclassification including high PSA density, a high number of positive 
cores (eg, â„3), high gene expression risk (from tissue-based molecular 
tumor analysis).285-287 Of note, core involvement in the major active 
surveillance cohort studies was generally low (see Table 1 below) and 
very few patients with grade group 2 disease were included. Therefore, in 
very select patients with low-risk prostate cancer, upfront treatment with 
radical prostatectomy or RT may be appropriate based on shared 
decision-making with the patient. Patient anxiety should be addressed so 
that it is not a deterrent to the appropriate use of active surveillance.
Active Surveillance in Favorable Intermediate-Risk Disease 
The literature on outcomes of active surveillance in patients with 
intermediate-risk prostate cancer is limited because few prospective 
studies of active surveillance included patients with intermediate-risk 
prostate cancer (see Table 1, below).
In prospective studies that included patients with intermediate-risk 
disease, favorable prostate cancer-specific survival rates of 94% to 100% 
were seen for the full cohorts.272,274,276 However, with extended follow-up, 
the Toronto group has demonstrated inferior metastasis-free survival for 
patients with intermediate-risk prostate cancer (15-year metastasis-free 
survival for cases of Gleason 6 or less with PSA <10 ng/mL, 94%;
Gleason 6 or less with PSA 10â20 ng/mL, 94%; Gleason 3+4 with PSA 20 
ng/mL or less, 84%; and Gleason 4+3 with PSA 20 ng/mL or less, 63%).288 
Furthermore, there was an unacceptably high 15-year distant metastasis 
rate (16.4%) that largely occurred between 10 to 15 years after 
diagnosis.288 Likewise, Canary PASS included a small subset of patients 
with grade group 2 disease, but only 2 patients had 15 years of follow-up. 
Two patients with grade group 2 prostate cancer developed metastatic 
disease between 9 and 12 years,289 with an estimated 15-year risk of 
distant metastasis of approximately 10%.284 Thus, 5 to 10 years of follow-
up may be insufficient to assess active surveillance safety.
Overall, the Panel interpreted these data to show that a subset of patients 
with favorable intermediate-risk prostate cancer and life expectancy >10 
years may be considered for active surveillance, especially with grade 
group 1 disease. However, the precise inclusion criteria and follow-up 
protocols need continued refinement. Patients must understand that a 
significant proportion of those clinically staged as having favorable 
intermediate-risk prostate cancer may have higher risk disease.290-293 
Particular consideration to active surveillance may be appropriate for 
those patients with a low percentage of Gleason pattern 4 cancer, low 
tumor volume, low PSA density, and/or low genomic risk (from tissue-
based molecular tumor analysis), but should be approached with caution, 
include informed decision-making, and use close monitoring for 
progression. How to optimize active surveillance for patients with grade 
group 2 prostate cancer remains an active area of investigation.
Confirmatory Testing
Confirmatory testing is critical to the process of active surveillance 
because a subset of patients will be found to no longer be candidates for 
active surveillance after confirmatory testing. Active surveillance starts 
after confirmatory testing. Since an initial prostate biopsy may 
underestimate tumor grade or volume, confirmatory testing is strongly

MS-21
recommended within the first 6 to 12 months of diagnosis for patients who 
are considering active surveillance.
Before starting on an active surveillance program, mpMRI with calculation 
of PSA density should be considered to confirm candidacy for active 
surveillance if not performed during initial workup.294 Patients with PI-
RADS 4 or 5 on mpMRI have an increased risk of biopsy progression 
during active surveillance.295
In patients with low- and favorable-intermediate-risk disease, molecular 
tumor analysis has not been shown in prospective studies to clearly 
improve long-term outcomes for patients receiving active surveillance. 
One study examined the role of molecular tumor analysis for predicting 
upgrading on surveillance biopsy or the presence of adverse pathology on 
eventual radical prostatectomy in patients in an active surveillance 
cohort.177 In this study, results of the molecular testing did not significantly 
improve risk stratification over the use of clinical variables alone.
If results of mpMRI and/or molecular testing are concerning, a repeat 
biopsy may be appropriate.
Early confirmatory testing may not be necessary in patients who have had 
a complete workup including mpMRI prior to diagnostic biopsy. However, 
all patients should undergo a confirmatory prostate biopsy within 1 to 2 
years of their diagnostic biopsy.
Active Surveillance Program
The current Oncology Guidelines recommendations for the active surveillance program 
include PSA no more often than every 6 months unless clinically indicated; 
DRE no more often than every 12 months unless clinically indicated; 
repeat prostate biopsy no more often than every 12 months unless 
clinically indicated; and consideration of repeat mpMRI no more often than 
every 12 months unless clinically indicated. mpMRI may be particularly
useful to exclude the presence of anterior cancer when PSA levels 
increase and systematic prostate biopsies remain negative.296
Results of a study of 211 patients with Grade Group 1 prostate cancer 
who had initial and repeat mpMRIs and PSA monitoring suggest that a 
negative initial mpMRI predicts a low risk of Gleason upgrading by 
systematic biopsy.297 In addition, PSA velocity was significantly associated 
with subsequent progression in those with an initial negative mpMRI. In 
contrast, those with high-risk visible lesions on mpMRI before initiation of 
active surveillance had an increased risk of progression. A meta-analysis 
of 43 studies found the sensitivity and NPV for mpMRI to be 0.81 and 
0.78, respectively.298 An analysis of patients in Canary PASS found that 
mpMRI had an NPV and PPV for detecting Grade Group â„2 cancer of 
83% and 31%, respectively.299 Another study found the NPV of mpMRI to 
be 80%.300
In patients with a suspicious lesion on mpMRI, MRI-US fusion biopsy 
improves the detection of higher grade (Grade Group â„2) cancers. Early 
experience supports the utilization of mpMRI in biopsy protocols to better 
risk stratify patients under active surveillance.301-303 However, more recent 
studies have shown that a significant proportion of high-grade cancers are 
detected with systematic biopsy and not targeted biopsy in patients on 
active surveillance.304-306
Whereas the intensity of surveillance may be tailored on an individual 
basis (eg, based on life expectancy and risk of reclassification), most 
patients should have prostate biopsies incorporated as part of their 
monitoring, but no more often than every 12 months, because PSA 
kinetics may not be reliable for predicting progression. Repeat biopsy is 
useful to determine whether higher Gleason grade exists, which may 
influence prognosis and hence the decision to continue active surveillance 
or proceed to definitive local therapy.307 A repeat prostate biopsy should 
also be considered if the prostate exam changes, if mpMRI (if done)

Prostate Cancer
MS-22
suggests more aggressive disease, or if PSA increases. However, 
literature suggests that as many as 5% of patients undergoing prostate 
biopsy will suffer an adverse event, including serious ones such as 
infections requiring hospitalization.308-310 Therefore, many clinicians 
choose to wait 2 years for a biopsy if there are no signs of progression.
Patients should be transitioned to observation (see Observation, above) 
when life expectancy is <10 years and they have low risk disease.
Considerations for Treatment of Patients on Active Surveillance
Reliable parameters of prostate cancer progression await the results of 
ongoing clinical trials. PSADT is not considered reliable enough to be used 
alone to detect disease progression.311 If repeat biopsy shows Grade 
Group â„3 disease, or if tumor is found in a greater number of biopsy cores 
or in a higher percentage of a given biopsy core, cancer progression may 
have occurred. Grade reclassification on repeat biopsy is the most 
common factor influencing a change in management approach from active 
surveillance to treatment. Other factors affecting decisions to actively treat 
patients on active surveillance include increase in tumor volume and a rise 
in PSA density. Considerations for a change in management strategy 
should be made in the context of the patientâs life expectancy. Patient 
anxiety over continued active surveillance should be addressed if 
treatment does not appear warranted.
Each of the major active surveillance series has used different criteria for 
reclassification.272,275,278-281,312-315 Reclassification criteria were met by 23% 
of patients with a median follow-up of 7 years in the Toronto experience,313 
36% of patients with a median follow-up of 5 years in the Johns Hopkins 
experience,275 and 16% of patients with a median follow-up of 3.5 years in 
the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) experience281 (also see 
Table 1, below). Uncertainty regarding reclassification criteria and the 
desire to avoid missing an opportunity for cure drove several reports that
dealt with the validity of commonly used reclassification criteria. The 
Toronto group demonstrated that a PSA trigger point of PSADT <3 years 
could not be improved upon by using a PSA threshold of 10 or 20, PSADT 
calculated in various ways, or PSA velocity >2 ng/mL/y.316 The Johns 
Hopkins group used biopsy-demonstrated reclassification to Gleason 
pattern 4 or 5 or increased tumor volume on biopsy as their criteria for 
reclassification. Of 290 patients on an annual prostate biopsy program, 
35% demonstrated reclassification at a median follow-up of 2.9 years.317 
Neither PSADT (area under the curve [AUC], 0.59) nor PSA velocity 
(AUC, 0.61) was associated with prostate biopsy reclassification. Both 
groups have concluded that PSA kinetics cannot replace regular prostate 
biopsy, although treatment of most patients who demonstrate 
reclassification on prostate biopsy prevents evaluation of biopsy 
reclassification as a criterion for treatment or reduction of survival. 
Treatment of all patients who developed Gleason pattern 4 on annual 
prostate biopsies has thus far resulted in only two prostate cancer deaths 
among 1298 patients (0.15%) in the Johns Hopkins study.275 However, it 
remains uncertain whether treatment of all who progressed to Gleason 
pattern 4 was necessary. Studies remain in progress to identify the best 
trigger points when interventions with curative intent may still be 
successful.
The patients in the Toronto cohort were followed for a median of 6.8 
years.313 Of the 30% (n = 145) of patients who progressed, 8% had an 
increase in Gleason grade, 14% had a PSADT <3 years, 1% developed a 
prostate nodule, and 3% were treated because of anxiety. One hundred 
thirty-five of these 145 patients were treated: 35 by radical prostatectomy, 
90 by external beam RT (EBRT) with or without ADT, and 10 with ADT 
alone. The 10- and 15-year actuarial cause-specific survival rates for the 
entire cohort were 98.1% and 94.3%, respectively.272 Only 15 of 993 
(1.5%) patients had died of prostate cancer, an additional 13 patients 
(1.3%) had developed metastatic disease, and only 36.5% of the cohort

Prostate Cancer
MS-23
had received treatment by 10 years. In an analysis of 592 patients enrolled 
in this cohort who had â„1 repeat prostate biopsies, 31.3% of cases were 
upgraded. Fifteen percent of upgraded cases were upgraded to Gleason 
â„8, and 62% of total upgraded cases proceeded to active treatment.318 
Another analysis of this cohort revealed that metastatic disease developed 
in 13 of 133 patients with Gleason 7 disease (9.8%) and 17 of 847 patients 
with Gleason â€6 disease (2.0%).319 PSADT and the number of positive 
scores were also predictors of increased risk for the development of 
metastatic disease.
In comparison, among 192 patients on active surveillance who underwent 
delayed treatment at a median of 2 years after diagnosis in the Johns 
Hopkins experience, 5-year biochemical PFS was 96% for those who 
underwent radical prostatectomy and 75% for those who underwent 
radiation.315 The two groups were similar by pathologic Gleason grade, 
pathologic stage, and margin positivity. All patients treated by radical 
prostatectomy after progression on active surveillance had freedom from 
biochemical progression at a median follow-up of 37.5 months, compared 
to 97% of those in the primary radical prostatectomy group at a median 
follow-up of 35.5 months. A later publication from this group showed that 
23 of 287 patients who were treated after active surveillance (8%) 
experienced biochemical recurrence, and the rate was independent of the 
type of treatment.275 Several studies have shown that delayed radical 
prostatectomy does not increase the rates of adverse pathology.279,320-322
Radical Prostatectomy
Radical prostatectomy is an appropriate option for patients whose cancer 
appears clinically localized to the prostate and can safely undergo general 
anesthesia and an operation. Because of potential perioperative morbidity, 
radical prostatectomy should generally be reserved for patients whose life 
expectancy is â„10 years.
Radical prostatectomy was compared to watchful waiting in a randomized 
trial of 695 patients with early-stage prostate cancer (mostly T2) in the 
SPCG-4 trial.247,323 With a median follow-up of 12.8 years, those assigned 
to the radical prostatectomy group had significant improvements in 
disease-specific survival, OS, and risk of metastasis and local 
progression.323 The reduction in mortality was confirmed at 18-year follow-
up, with an absolute difference of 11%.247 Overall, 8 patients needed to be 
treated to avert one death; that number fell to 4 for patients <65 years of 
age. Longer follow-up results were also reported, in which the cumulative 
incidence of death from prostate cancer was 19.6% and 31.3% in the 
radical prostatectomy and watchful waiting groups, respectively, at 23 
years, with a mean increase of 2.9 years of life in the radical 
prostatectomy group.248 However, in the small subset of patients with high-
risk disease, there was no improvement in survival between treatment 
groups. A report of 30-year follow-up of this trial showed that the absolute 
difference in death due to prostate cancer between groups increased over 
time.324 Radical prostatectomy resulted in a 48% lower risk of death from 
prostate cancer and a mean of 2.2 life-years gained. The authors 
concluded that treating six patients would result in one prostate cancer 
death averted. The results of this trial offer high-quality evidence to 
support radical prostatectomy alone as a treatment option for patients with 
intermediate-risk clinically localized prostate cancer.
While the SPCG-4 and PIVOT trials comparing observation to surgery did 
not clearly demonstrate an improvement in outcomes among those with 
high-risk disease, these trials enrolled few patients and are not adequately 
powered to rigorously assess the role of surgery in high-risk disease.248,249 
Trials that have utilized surgery in high-risk disease generally demonstrate 
a 70% to 80% risk of recurrence and thus surgery should be viewed as 
one component of a multi-modality approach.325 For example, in the 
CALGB 90203 trial of patients who received surgery in the control arm,

Prostate Cancer
MS-24
69% received adjuvant or secondary treatment, most commonly 
radiotherapy with or without ADT.
There remains no high-level evidence for the use of radical prostatectomy 
for patients with Oncology Guidelines very-high-risk, regional/node-positive, or 
metastatic disease. Ideally surgery is used in the context of a clinical trial 
or when radiotherapy plus ADT is contraindicated. Surgery should be 
discussed as one component of a planned multimodality approach in 
these patients. There is an ongoing trial, SPCG-15, which aims to 
compare RP versus RT plus ADT in patients with high-risk prostate 
cancer.326
Radical prostatectomy is also a treatment option for select patients 
experiencing a local recurrence after primary EBRT, but morbidity 
(incontinence, erectile dysfunction, bladder neck contracture, and rectal 
injury) remain significantly higher than when radical prostatectomy is used 
as initial therapy.327,328 Patient selection is important, and post-RT 
recurrence radical prostatectomy should only be performed by highly 
experienced surgeons.
Operative Techniques and Adverse Effects
The retropubic and the perineal approaches are reasonable to perform a 
radical prostatectomy. Surgeons who perform a high-volume of operations 
in high-volume centers generally achieve superior outcomes.329,330 
Laparoscopic and robot-assisted radical prostatectomy are commonly 
used and are considered comparable to conventional approaches in 
experienced hands.331-333 In a cohort study using SEER Medicare-linked 
data on 8837 patients, minimally invasive compared to open radical 
prostatectomy was associated with shorter length of hospital stay, less 
need for blood transfusions, and fewer surgical complications, but rates of 
incontinence and erectile dysfunction were higher.334 A second large study 
reported no difference in overall complications, readmission, and
additional cancer therapies between open and robot-assisted radical 
prostatectomy, although the robotic approach was associated with higher 
rates of genitourinary complications and lower rates of blood 
transfusion.335 Oncologic outcomes of a robotic versus open approach 
were similar when assessed by use of additional therapies334 or rate of 
positive surgical margins,336 although longer follow-up is necessary. A 
meta-analysis of 19 observational studies (n = 3893) reported less blood 
loss and lower transfusion rates with minimally invasive techniques than 
with open operation.336 Risk of positive surgical margins was the same. 
Two more recent meta-analyses showed a statistically significant 
advantage in favor of a robotic approach compared to an open approach 
in 12-month urinary continence337 and potency recovery.338 Early results 
from a randomized controlled phase 3 study comparing robot-assisted 
laparoscopic radical prostatectomy and open radical retropubic 
prostatectomy in 326 patients were published in 2016.339,340 Urinary 
function and sexual function scores and rates of postoperative 
complications did not differ significantly between the groups at 6, 12, and 
24 months after surgery. Rates of positive surgical margins were similar, 
based on a superiority test (10% in the open group vs. 15% in the robotic 
group). Assessment of oncologic outcomes from this trial will be limited 
because postoperative management and additional cancer therapies were 
not standardized between the groups.339
Return of urinary continence after radical prostatectomy may be improved 
by preserving the urethra beyond the prostatic apex and by avoiding 
damage to the distal sphincter mechanism. Bladder neck preservation 
may allow more rapid recovery of urinary control.341 Anastomotic strictures 
that increase the risk of long-term incontinence are less frequent with 
modern surgical techniques. Recovery of erectile function is related 
directly to the degree of preservation of the cavernous nerves, age at 
surgery, and preoperative erectile function. Improvement in urinary and 
sexual function has been reported with nerve-sparing techniques.342,343

Prostate Cancer
MS-25
Replacement of resected nerves with nerve grafts does not appear to be 
effective for patients undergoing wide resection of the neurovascular 
bundles.344 The ability of mpMRI to detect extracapsular extension can aid 
in decision-making in nerve-sparing surgery.201
Common adverse events after RP include urinary incontinence, erectile 
dysfunction, penile shortening, and urethral or bladder neck contracture or 
stricture. The rates of occurrence vary by patient population (eg, by age 
and risk group), surgeon skill, and surgical technique (eg, unilateral vs. 
bilateral nerve sparring). In the ProtecT trial and PACE-A, which both 
randomized patients to RP versus RT, RP was associated with 40% to 
60% risk of erectile dysfunction and 20% to 50% risk of urinary 
incontinence requiring at least one pad per day.25,345
Pelvic Lymph Node Dissection
PLND can be considered with a radical prostatectomy for patients with 
favorable intermediate, unfavorable intermediate, high, or very-high-risk 
prostate cancer. If performed, it is recommended to be an extended 
PLND.346,347 An extended PLND includes removal of all node-bearing 
tissue from an area bounded by the external iliac vein anteriorly, the pelvic 
side wall laterally, the bladder wall medially, the floor of the pelvis 
posteriorly, Cooperâs ligament distally, and the internal iliac artery 
proximally. Removal of more lymph nodes using the extended technique 
has been associated with increased likelihood of finding lymph node 
metastases, thereby providing more complete staging.348-350 A survival 
advantage with more extensive lymphadenectomy has been suggested by 
several studies, possibly due to elimination of microscopic 
metastases,349,351-353 although definitive proof of oncologic benefit is 
lacking.354 PLND can be performed safely laparoscopically, robotically, or 
as an open procedure, and complication rates should be similar among 
the three approaches.
Radiation Therapy
RT techniques used in prostate cancer can be described generally by the 
following: delivery method (ie, internal vs. EBRT), source of radiation (ie, 
photon vs. proton), planning technique (ie, 3D vs. intensity modulated RT 
[IMRT]), image guidance (none vs. cone beam CT [CBCT] or MRI), 
adaptation (ie, online adaptive treatment), and fractionation (ie, 
conventional vs. moderate hypofractionation vs. stereotactic body 
radiotherapy).
External Beam Radiation Therapy
Over the past several decades, EBRT techniques have evolved to allow 
higher doses of radiation to be administered safely. EBRT can be 
delivered either with photons, also called x-rays, or proton beam. Photons 
are used in >95% of cases in the United States.
Proton Therapy  
Proton therapy and x-rayâbased therapies like IMRT can deliver highly 
conformal doses to the prostate. Proton-based therapies will deliver less 
low-dose radiation to some of the surrounding normal tissues not 
immediately adjacent to the prostate, such as muscle, bone, vessels, and 
fat. These tissues do not routinely contribute to the morbidity of prostate 
radiation and are relatively resilient to radiation injury; therefore, the 
benefit of decreased dose to these types of normal, non-critical tissues 
has not been clearly proven.
Only one randomized trial has been performed, the PartiQOL trial, and 
results are reported in abstract only.355 This trial randomized 450 patients 
with low- and intermediate-risk prostate cancer to either proton or photons. 
After a median follow-up of 5-years, there were no significant differences 
in patient-reported quality of life, physician reported toxicity, or biochemical 
recurrence. Thus, both treatments appear to offer excellent tumor control 
and good safety profile.

Prostate Cancer
MS-26
The costs associated with proton beam facility construction and proton 
beam treatment are high compared to the expense of building and using 
the more common photon linear accelerator-based practice.356 These 
higher costs may translate into higher charges for protons, a point that 
should be considered when tumor control and side effects appear similar 
between photon and proton beam therapy for prostate cancer, especially if 
these charges are incurred by the patient.
The Oncology Guidelines Panel believes no clear evidence supports a benefit or 
decrement to proton therapy over IMRT for either treatment efficacy or 
long-term toxicity. Conventionally fractionated or moderately 
hypofractionated prostate proton therapy can be considered a reasonable 
alternative to x-rayâbased regimens at clinics with appropriate technology, 
physics, and clinical expertise. Insufficient data is present to recommend 
their delivery with ultra-hypofractionation.
Photon (X-ray) Beam 
Treatment planning in the 1980s used two-dimensional planning 
techniques. By the 1990s this was largely replaced by three-dimensional 
planning. In the late 1990s IMRT planning techniques were increasingly 
utilized, and as of 2010 nearly all centers utilize IMRT planning. This 
technique has been shown to improve dose conformality.
In parallel, improvements in image guidance, also referred to as image 
guided RT (IGRT), have occurred. The purpose of IGRT is to ensure the 
planned dose is delivered as intended. Given the potential changes in 
anatomy day to day due to bladder and rectal filling, IGRT is essential to 
ensure accurate alignment of the prostate and target volumes and 
avoidance of organs at risk. IGRT techniques often include use of 
implanted fiducials, electromagnetic targeting and tracking, or endorectal 
balloon, which have variably been shown to decrease complications and 
allow margin reduction.357 More recently, the use of real-time tracking and 
online adaptive radiotherapy have also demonstrated the ability to improve
dosimetry and enable margin reduction, which may result in reduced 
toxicity.358
The advancements in treatment planning and IGRT have enabled greater 
comfort and confidence to test increasingly hypofractionated regimens. 
Historically, conventionally fractionated RT was utilized with 1.8 to 2.0 Gy 
per fraction over 8 to 9 weeks of daily RT. This schema has been 
compared in numerous randomized trials to moderately hypofractionated 
RT in 2.5 to 4 Gy/fraction, which has been shown to be noninferior when 
using iso-effective doses.359-368 Recently, the HYDRA individual patient 
meta-analyses of these trials demonstrated that 60 Gy in 20 fractions, or 
regimens with a similar iso-effective dose, should be preferred.369 
Reduction from >40 treatments to 20 treatments reduces both financial 
toxicity and increases patient convenience.
The relatively slow proliferation rate of prostate cancer is reflected in a low 
Î±/ÎČ ratio, most commonly reported between 1 and 4.370 Because the Î±/ÎČ 
ratio for prostate cancer is similar to or lower than the surrounding tissues 
responsible for most of the toxicity reported with radiation, appropriately 
designed radiation treatment fields and schedules using ultra 
hypofractionated regimens were hypothesized to result in similar cancer 
control rates without increased risk of late toxicity.
SBRT is a technique that delivers highly conformal, high-dose radiation in 
five or fewer treatment fractions, which are safe to administer only with 
precise, image-guided delivery.371 Numerous prospective studies 
demonstrated safety and efficacy of SBRT in the setting of localized 
prostate cancer.372 Two large randomized phase 3 trials have reported 
results. HYPO-RT-PC trial enrolled 1200 patients with intermediate- and 
high-risk prostate cancer and demonstrated noninferiority of 42.7 Gy in 
seven fractions to 78.0 Gy in 39 fractions with respect to FFS.373 Likewise, 
PACE-B compared 5-fraction SBRT with a dose of 40 Gy to the prostate 
and 36.25 Gy to the planning target volume versus moderate

hypofractionated RT with 62 Gy in 20 fractions.374 Results showed 
noninferiority in tumor control and generally similar safety profiles.
SBRT regimens should utilize IMRT planning and daily IGRT. SBRT is an 
appropriate standard RT method at clinics with appropriate technology, 
physics, and clinical expertise.
The Panel lists fractionation schemes that have shown acceptable efficacy 
and toxicity within the Principles of Radiation Therapy.
Relative contraindications or considerations to EBRT include prior pelvic 
irradiation, active inflammatory disease of the rectum, permanent 
indwelling Foley catheter, very low bladder capacity, chronic moderate or 
severe diarrhea, and bladder outlet obstruction requiring a suprapubic 
catheter.
Hydrogel Per-Rectal Spacers 
Biomaterials have been developed, tested, and FDA approved to serve as 
spacer materials when inserted between the rectum and prostate.375,376 In 
a randomized phase 3 multicenter clinical trial of patients undergoing 
image-guided IMRT (IG-IMRT), the risk of late (3-year) grade 2 or higher, 
physician-recorded rectal complications declined from 5.7% to 0% in the 
control versus hydrogel spacer group.377 The control group had a 
significant reduction in bowel QOL decline. No significant differences in 
urinary adverse events were noted in those receiving hydrogel placement 
versus controls. Results of a secondary analysis of this trial suggest that 
use of a perirectal spacer may decrease the sexual side effects of 
radiation.378 Spacer implantation may be associated with rare 
complications such as rectum perforation and urethral damage.379,380 
Retrospective data also support its use in similar patients undergoing 
brachytherapy. Overall, the Panel believes that biocompatible and 
biodegradable perirectal spacer materials may be implanted between the 
prostate and rectum in patients undergoing RT in order to displace the
rectum from high radiation dose regions. Patients with obvious rectal 
invasion or visible posterior extension should not undergo perirectal 
spacer implantation.
EBRT for Low-Risk Disease 
Active surveillance is preferred for low-risk disease. If EBRT is to be used, 
it should be given as monotherapy without a brachytherapy boost, without 
nodal irradiation, and without ADT. Ideally it is given with SBRT or 
moderately hypofractionated RT.
EBRT for Intermediate-Risk Disease 
The favorable/unfavorable distinction within the intermediate-risk group 
should be used to help guide treatment for these patients. Generally, 
patients with favorable intermediate-risk disease should be treated with 
EBRT alone without a brachytherapy boost, without nodal irradiation, and 
without ADT.381 Use of SBRT or moderately hypofractionated RT are 
preferred. Patients with unfavorable intermediate-risk disease generally 
should be considered for the addition of short-term ADT.382,383 Studies 
have shown that the addition of a focal EBRT boost may further improve 
biochemical control in these patients.384 Similarly, the addition of a 
brachytherapy boost may improve biochemical control, at the expense of 
increased toxicity.385 Trials to date that have enrolled patients with 
intermediate-risk disease have not shown improvements in disease control 
from the use of elective nodal irradiation (ENI), and have generally seen 
an increase in toxicity.386
EBRT for Patients with High-Risk or Very-High-Risk Disease 
EBRT represents the only local therapy for patients with high- or very-
high-risk disease that has been shown to improve overall survival when 
compared to ADT alone.387-390 Furthermore, the addition of ADT, 
specifically long-term ADT of >18 months, has been shown to improve 
overall survival as compared to RT alone or RT with short-term ADT.382

Prostate Cancer
MS-28
There are mixed results regarding the benefit of ENI in this patient 
population. Three multicenter cooperative group trials have not 
demonstrated improvement in outcomes from ENI; GETUG-01, RTOG 
9413, and recently in abstract form RTOG 0924.386,391,392 However, one 
small single center trial from India, POP-RT, showed that pelvic radiation 
can improve biochemical FFS and DFS compared with prostate-only 
radiation in patients with high- and very-high-risk prostate cancer.393 With 
the recent results of NRG/RTOG 0924 (ClinicalTrials.gov ID 
NCT01368588) demonstrating no improvement in distant metastasis, 
prostate cancer-specific mortality, or overall survival from ENI in an 
approximately 2500 patient trial of unfavorable-intermediate and high-risk 
prostate cancer, the role of ENI remains unclear.
More recently, the STAMPEDE trial has reported a post-hoc analysis of 
the benefit of adding abiraterone acetate/prednisone to EBRT plus long-
term ADT in patients with Oncology Guidelines very-high-risk prostate cancer.394 Results 
demonstrated improvement in metastasis-free survival from the addition of 
abiraterone at the expense of increased grade â„3 side effects. Notably, 
the patients enrolled in this trial had markedly higher rates of metastasis 
and death from prostate cancer than those observed in other trials of high-
risk prostate cancer conducted by NRG, EORTC, or TROG. Given some 
patients had PSAs >1000 ng/mL on the STAMPEDE trial but were called 
non-metastatic, it remains unclear the true value of the addition of 
abiraterone in contemporary very-high-risk disease. ENZARAD, a 
randomized trial of patients with high- and very-high-risk and clinical node 
positive disease, recently reported results in abstract form and 
demonstrated that the addition of the APRI enzalutamide to RT+ADT did 
not improve metastasis-free survival or overall survival.395 Ongoing trials 
utilizing other ARPIs have not yet reported their results, such as ATLAS 
(NCT02531516).
EBRT for Node-Positive Disease 
EBRT with neoadjuvant, concurrent, and/or adjuvant ADT and abiraterone 
is the preferred option for patients with clinical N1 disease based upon the 
previously discussed STAMPEDE trial.394 Other options in this setting 
include EBRT with ADT, ADT alone or with abiraterone, and, for select 
patients, RP plus PLND (see Radical Prostatectomy, above).
Post-Recurrence EBRT 
The use of EBRT in patients with biochemical recurrence is discussed 
under Post-Radical Prostatectomy Approaches, below.
Brachytherapy
Brachytherapy involves placing radioactive sources into the prostate 
tissue. Brachytherapy has been used traditionally for low-risk disease 
because earlier studies found it less effective than EBRT for high-risk 
disease.121,396 However, increasing evidence suggests that technical 
advancements in brachytherapy may provide a role for contemporary 
brachytherapy in higher-risk localized and locally advanced prostate 
cancer.397
There are currently two methods for prostate brachytherapy: low dose-rate 
(LDR) and high dose-rate (HDR). LDR brachytherapy consists of 
placement of permanent seed implants in the prostate. The short range of 
the radiation emitted from these low-energy sources allows delivery of 
adequate dose levels to the cancer within the prostate, with excessive 
irradiation of the bladder and rectum avoided. Post-implant dosimetry 
should be performed to document the quality of an LDR implant.398 HDR 
brachytherapy, which involves temporary insertion of a radiation source, is 
a newer approach.
Two groups have observed a lower risk of urinary frequency, urgency, and 
rectal pain with HDR brachytherapy compared with LDR brachytherapy 
(permanent seed implant).399,400 Vargas and colleagues401 reported that

HDR brachytherapy results in a lower risk of erectile dysfunction than LDR 
brachytherapy. Commonly prescribed doses for LDR and HDR 
brachytherapy are listed in the Principles of Radiation Therapy within the 
algorithm above.
For patients with very large or very small prostates, symptoms of bladder 
outlet obstruction (high International Prostate Symptom Score), or a 
previous transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP), seed implantation 
may be more difficult. These patients also have an increased risk of side 
effects. Neoadjuvant ADT may be used to shrink the prostate to an 
acceptable size; however, increased toxicity is expected from ADT, and 
prostate size may not decline in some patients. The potential toxicity of 
ADT must be weighed against the possible benefit of target reduction.
Ideally, the accuracy of brachytherapy treatment should be verified by 
daily prostate localization with techniques of IGRT: CT, ultrasound, 
implanted fiducials, or electromagnetic targeting/tracking. Endorectal 
balloons may be used to improve prostate immobilization. Perirectal 
spacer materials (discussed under External Beam Radiation Therapy, 
above) may be used when the previously mentioned techniques are 
insufficient to reduce side effects due to anatomic geometry or other 
patient-related factors (eg, medication usage, comorbid conditions). 
Patients with obvious rectal invasion or visible cT3 and posterior extension 
should not undergo perirectal spacer implantation.
Brachytherapy Alone for Localized Disease 
Active surveillance is preferred for low-risk disease, but brachytherapy 
alone is an option for select patients with low-risk disease. Brachytherapy 
alone is an option for patients with favorable intermediate-risk and for 
carefully selected patients with unfavorable intermediate-risk prostate 
cancer, depending on life expectancy. Patients with high-risk cancers are 
not generally considered candidates for brachytherapy alone. Either LDR 
or HDR brachytherapy can be used in this setting; however, there are
concerns regarding the efficacy of both single fraction HDR and, more 
recently, two fraction HDR as monotherapy.402
There are no reported trials comparing SBRT or other contemporary 
EBRT methods to brachytherapy. ASCENDE-SBRT is comparing a 
brachytherapy boost versus SBRT.403
Brachytherapy Boost  
LDR or HDR brachytherapy can be added as a boost to EBRT plus ADT in 
patients with unfavorable intermediate- or high-risk prostate cancer being 
treated with curative intent and for carefully selected patients with very-
high-risk disease. Combining EBRT and brachytherapy allows dose 
escalation. This combination has demonstrated improved biochemical 
control over EBRT plus ADT alone in randomized trials, but with higher 
toxicity.404-406
The randomized ASCENDE-RT trial compared two methods of dose 
escalation in 398 patients with intermediate- or high-risk prostate cancer: 
dose-escalated EBRT boost to 78 Gy or LDR brachytherapy boost.385 All 
patients were initially treated with 12 months of ADT and pelvic EBRT to 
46 Gy. An ITT analysis found that the primary endpoint of biochemical 
PFS was 86% versus 75% at 7 years for the LDR boost versus EBRT 
boost arms (log-rank P < .001). Toxicity was higher in the brachytherapy 
arm, with the cumulative incidence of grade 3 genitourinary events at 5 
years of 18.4% for brachytherapy boost and 5.2% for EBRT boost (P < 
.001).407 A trend for increased gastrointestinal toxicity with brachytherapy 
boost was also seen (cumulative incidence of grade 3 events at 5 years, 
8.1% vs. 3.2%; P = .12). However, at 6-year follow-up, health-related QOL 
was similar between the groups in most domains, except that physical and 
urinary function scales were significantly worse in the LDR arm.408 
Whereas the toxicity is increased with the use of brachytherapy boost, this 
and other randomized controlled trials have not shown an improvement in 
OS or cancer-specific survival.409

Prostate Cancer

The optimal duration of ADT with EBRT plus brachytherapy is unclear. 
The TROG RADAR trial randomized patients to receive 6 versus 18 
months of ADT, and a subset of participants received EBRT with an HDR 
boost.410 In the subset who received EBRT plus HDR, the use of 18 
months of ADT improved overall survival compared to 6 months. More 
recently, the TRIP/TRIGU0907 randomized trial of EBRT plus LDR 
compared 6 versus 30 months of ADT.411 The study found no significant 
difference in biochemical progression between arms, both were <10% at 
7-year follow-up.
To address concerns for increased toxicity incidence associated with 
brachytherapy boost from historical trial data, careful patient selection and 
contemporary planning associated with lesser toxicity, such as use of 
recognized organ at risk dose constraints, use of high-quality ultrasound 
and other imaging, and prescription of dose as close as possible to the 
target without excessive margins, should be implemented.
Post-Recurrence Brachytherapy 
Brachytherapy can be considered in patients with biochemical recurrence 
after EBRT. Results of a phase 2 study of post-recurrence HDR 
brachytherapy after EBRT included relapse-free survival, distant 
metastases-free survival, and cause-specific survival rates of 68.5%, 
81.5%, and 90.3%, respectively, at 5 years.412 Toxicities were mostly 
grade 1 and 2 and included gastrointestinal toxicity and urethral strictures, 
and one case of Grade 3 urinary incontinence. In another prospective 
phase 2 trial, the primary endpoint of grade â„3 late treatment-related 
gastrointestinal and genitourinary adverse events at 9 to 24 months after 
post-recurrence brachytherapy was below the unacceptable threshold, at 
14%.413
Data on the use of brachytherapy after permanent brachytherapy are 
limited, but the Panel agrees that it can be considered for carefully 
selected patients. Decisions regarding the use of brachytherapy in the
recurrent-disease setting should consider comorbidities, extent of disease, 
and potential complications. Brachytherapy in this setting is best 
performed at high-volume centers.
Radiation for Distant Metastases
EBRT can be used for multiple purposes in the setting of metastatic 
disease. Historically, it was only used as a means of palliation, often for 
bone pain, obstruction, or bleeding. A short course of 8 Gy x 1 is as 
effective for palliation as, and less costly than, 30 Gy in 10 fractions.414 In 
a randomized trial of 898 patients with bone metastases, grade 2â4 acute 
toxicity was observed less often in the 8-Gy arm (10%) than in the 30-Gy 
arm (17%) (P = .002); however, the retreatment rate was higher in the 8-
Gy group (18%) than in the 30-Gy group (9%) (P < .001).415 In another 
study of 425 patients with painful bone metastases, a single dose of 8 Gy 
was noninferior to 20 Gy in multiple fractions in terms of overall pain 
response to treatment.416 The SCORAD randomized trial did not show 
noninferiority for ambulatory status of single-fraction 8-Gy EBRT to 20 Gy 
in 5 fractions.417
Eight Gy as a single dose is as effective for short-term (ie, 12 week) pain 
palliation at any bony site as longer courses of radiation, but re-treatment 
rates are higher. Other regimens (eg, 30 Gy in 10 fractions) may be used 
as alternative palliative dosing depending on clinical scenario. Neither 
regimen has demonstrated clear long-term pain or cancer control and are 
intended for transient pain control palliation.
EBRT may also be used as an oncologic treatment and not solely for the 
palliation of symptoms in the metastatic setting. The topic of metastasis-
directed radiation therapy is discussed in detail below (see MDT for 
Oligometastatic CSPC and MDT for mCRPC).

Oncology Guidelines Version 5.2026 
Prostate Cancer
MS-31
Comparison of Treatment Options for Localized Disease
Multiple randomized trials have compared RT to RP. The ProtecT trial 
demonstrated that, with a median follow-up of 15 years, patients with low 
and intermediate risk prostate cancer had similar rates of developing 
metastasis or death.263,265 A post-hoc subset analysis of the ProtecT trial 
demonstrated that patients with cribriform features on active monitoring 
had significantly reduced rates of distant metastasis when treated with RT 
plus ADT, but not with RP.264
The ProtecT trial demonstrated that urinary incontinence rates and 
impotence rates were significantly higher with RP than RT. There were 
small but significant increases in bowel side effects with RT as compared 
to surgery. The RT utilized on this trial was 3D conformal radiotherapy 
without contemporary image guidance or rectal spacer use.
More recently, results from PACE-A, which included patients with 
intermediate-risk prostate cancer receiving either SBRT versus RP, were 
reported.345 No spacer gels were utilized. The study demonstrated that, 
with more modern RT, there were smaller differences in patient-reported 
bowel quality of life as compared to surgery. Furthermore, those receiving 
surgery were >3-fold more likely to develop impotence, and >8-fold more 
likely to develop urinary incontinence requiring â„1 pads per day.
Recently, a cross-trial comparison using two phase 3 randomized trials 
was performed.418 Trials were matched by era, country, and 
randomization. NRG/RTOG 0521 randomized patients with high-risk 
prostate cancer to RT plus 24 months of ADT with or without 6 cycles of 
docetaxel, and CALGB randomized patients with high-risk prostate cancer 
to surgery with or without 6 cycles of docetaxel with concurrent ADT in the 
neoadjuvant setting. The patients in the RT trial were generally older and 
had more aggressive prognostic features. This study demonstrated that 
patients receiving RT with ADT had significantly reduced risk of
developing distant metastasis than patients treated with a surgical 
approach, but there were no significant differences in death from prostate 
cancer. As this was not a true randomized trial of the two treatment 
approaches, confounding may impact the results.
Numerous non-randomized studies have been reported that should be 
interpreted with caution given the high risk for selection bias and residual 
confounding. Barocas et al compared radical prostatectomy, EBRT, and 
active surveillance in 2550 patients and found that, after 3 years, radical 
prostatectomy was associated with a greater worsening in urinary and 
sexual function than either EBRT or active surveillance.419 However, 
radical prostatectomy was also associated with an improvement in urinary 
irritative symptoms that was not observed with radiation or active 
surveillance. Health-related QOL measures including bowel and hormonal 
function were similar among the groups, as was disease-specific survival.
Chen et al compared radical prostatectomy, EBRT, and brachytherapy 
against active surveillance in 1141 patients.23 As in the Barocas study, 
radical prostatectomy was associated with greater declines in sexual and 
urinary function than other treatments at 3 months. In this study, EBRT 
was associated with worse short-term bowel function, and both EBRT and 
brachytherapy were associated with worsened urinary obstructive and 
irritative symptoms. By 2 years, however, differences among the groups 
compared with active surveillance were insignificant. Results of a 
systematic review showed similar findings to these studies.420
Another study examined patient-reported outcomes in >2000 patients with 
localized prostate cancer managed by radical prostatectomy, 
brachytherapy, EBRT with or without ADT, or active surveillance.24 By 5 
years, most functional differences were minimal between management 
approaches. However, radical prostatectomy was associated with worse 
incontinence in the full cohort and with worse sexual function in those with
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by MĂĄximo LĂłpez Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:29:25 PM. Copyright Â© 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 5.2026 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂ© (Oncology GuidelinesÂ©), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 5.2026 
Prostate Cancer
MS-32
unfavorable intermediate-, high-, or very-high-risk disease than in those 
treated with EBRT and ADT.
Other Local Therapies
Many therapies have been investigated for the treatment of localized 
prostate. Cryotherapy was investigated as a potential whole gland 
treatment option. A study by Donnelly and colleagues included 244 
patients with T2 or T3 disease who were randomly assigned to either 
cryotherapy or EBRT.421 In this small trial, all patients received 
neoadjuvant ADT. Patients who received cryotherapy reported poorer 
sexual function.422 For patients with locally advanced cancer, cryoablation 
was associated with a lower 8-year biochemical progression-free rate 
compared to EBRT in a small trial of 62 patients.423
Other ablative therapies (eg, high-intensity focused ultrasound [HIFU], 
transurethral ultrasound ablation [TULSA], irreversible electroporation 
[IRE], photodynamic therapy [PDT]) are not recommended as primary 
therapy for localized prostate cancer due to lack of long-term randomized 
data comparing these treatments to radiation or radical prostatectomy.424-
430 Single arm phase I or II trials have shown high rates of residual or 
recurrent cancer (20%â70%) and clinically meaningful toxicity (ie, 
incontinence, obstruction, erectile dysfunction). Furthermore, monitoring 
strategies appear to be compromised (ie, MRI accuracy) and secondary 
treatment may carry higher risk of complications. These ablative 
treatments can be utilized in the context of a clinical trial, ideally a 
randomized trial compared them to a current standard treatment option. 
Companies and institutions may make nonâevidenced-based claims to the 
evidence, efficacy, and safety of focal therapy.
At this time, the only local therapy options that can be considered are 
cryotherapy, HIFU, and IRE (category 2B) in the setting of non-metastatic
RT recurrence due to the overall lack of high-quality evidence in this 
setting for any modality. Also see Post-Radiation Recurrence, below.
Disease Monitoring
Please refer to the Oncology Guidelines for Survivorship (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines) for recommendations regarding common consequences 
of cancer and cancer treatment (eg, anxiety, depression, trauma, and 
distress; hormone-related symptoms; sexual dysfunction) and on the 
promotion of physical activity, weight management, and proper 
immunizations in cancer survivors. Prostate-cancer specific survivorship 
recommendations pertaining to bone health and cardiovascular disease 
are included in the algorithm above and are discussed in more detail in the 
section on Adverse Effects of Traditional ADT, below.
Patients After Initial Definitive Therapy
For patients initially treated with curative intent, serum PSA levels should 
be measured every 6 to 12 months for the first 5 years and then annually. 
PSA testing every 3 months may be better for patients at high risk of 
recurrence. When prostate cancer recurred after radical prostatectomy, 
Pound and colleagues found that 45% of patients experienced recurrence 
within the first 2 years, 77% within the first 5 years, and 96% by 10 
years.431
Patients with N1 CSPC on ADT
The intensity of clinical monitoring for patients on ADT for N1 CSPC is 
determined by the response to initial ADT, EBRT, or both. Follow-up 
evaluation of these patients should include physical examination and PSA 
measurement every 3 to 6 months. Imaging for symptoms or increasing 
PSA. The relative risk for bone metastasis or death increases as PSADT 
falls. Bone imaging should be performed more frequently in patients with 
shorter PSADTs.432

Oncology Guidelines Version 5.2026 
Prostate Cancer
MS-33
Patients with Localized Disease Under Observation
Patients with localized disease on observation follow the same monitoring 
recommendations as patients with N1 CSPC who are on ADT.

header: Workup for Progression
content:
Castrate levels of testosterone should be documented if clinically indicated 
in patients with signs of progression, with adjustment of ADT as 
necessary. If serum testosterone levels are <50 ng/dL, the patient should 
undergo disease workup with bone and soft tissue imaging (see Imaging 
Techniques above for more details).
Post-Radical Prostatectomy Approaches
Patients who have undergone radical prostatectomy should be monitored 
as described above. However, some patients will have adverse pathologic 
features, positive lymph nodes, or biochemical persistence or recurrence. 
Options for these patients include adjuvant radiation (the use of EBRT 
immediately following radical prostatectomy in the setting of an 
undetectable postoperative PSA) with or without ADT, secondary radiation 
(the use of EBRT after radical prostatectomy when PSA is detectable) with 
or without ADT, or monitoring. Decisions about when to initiate post-
radical prostatectomy radiation and whether to include ADT for such 
patients are complex. The Panel recommends use of nomograms and 
consideration of age and comorbidities, clinical and pathologic information, 
PSA levels, and PSADT to individualize treatment discussion. The various 
scenarios are discussed in more detail below.
Bone and soft tissue imaging and prostate bed biopsy should be 
considered in patients with PSA persistence/recurrence after RP if their life 
expectancy is >5 years. The utility of imaging for patients with an early 
biochemical recurrence after radical prostatectomy depends on disease 
risk before operation and pathologic stage, Gleason grade, PSA, and 
PSADT after recurrence. Patients with low- and intermediate-risk disease
and low postoperative serum PSA levels have a very low risk of positive 
bone scans or CT scans.433,434 In a series of 414 bone scans performed in 
230 patients with biochemical recurrence after radical prostatectomy, the 
rate of a positive bone scan for patients with PSA >10 ng/mL was only 
4%.435 In another study, the probability of a positive bone scan for a 
patient not on ADT after radical prostatectomy was <5% unless the PSA 
increased to 40 to 45 ng/mL.436 Thus bone imaging is appropriate when 
patients develop symptoms or when PSA levels are increasing rapidly. A 
prostate bed biopsy can also be considered. However, PSMA PET/CT 
imaging has increased sensitivity at low PSA levels post-RP, and 
approximately 30% to 40% of patients will have detectable disease on 
PSMA PET/CT at PSA levels of 0.2 to 0.5 ng/mL.
Early Secondary or Adjuvant Treatment for Adverse Features After 
Radical Prostatectomy
Patients with undetectable PSA levels and without lymph node metastases 
following radical prostatectomy can be considered for immediate adjuvant 
treatment if adverse pathologic features are present (ie, positive margins, 
seminal vesicle invasion, extracapsular extension). However, monitoring 
these patients is the preferred option (category 1). Waiting for a detectable 
and rising PSA or PSA â„0.1 ng/mL before initiating secondary radiation is 
preferred in this setting, since this approach avoids over-treatment and 
subsequent potential side effects and does not appear to compromise 
oncologic outcomes.437,438
In the RADICALS-RT trial, 1396 patients with adverse features after 
radical prostatectomy were randomized to receive immediate adjuvant 
treatment or to be monitored with a policy to initiate secondary treatment if 
PSA levels reached 0.1 ng/mL or rose three consecutive times.439 
Participants were followed for a median 4.9 years and no differences were 
seen in 5-year biochemical PFS and freedom from non-protocol hormone 
therapy. There was no statistically significant difference in the primary
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by MĂĄximo LĂłpez Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:29:25 PM. Copyright Â© 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.

Oncology Guidelines Version 5.2026 
Prostate Cancer
MS-34
endpoint of freedom from distant metastasis between the arms at 10 years 
(93% in the adjuvant arm vs. 90% in the early secondary therapy arm; 
95% CI, 0.43â1.07; P = .095) and there was no difference in OS.438 
Importantly, urinary incontinence and grade 3â4 urethral strictures were 
more frequent in the adjuvant therapy group.
The GETUG-AFU 17 trial and the TROG 08.03/ANZUP RAVES trial were 
both terminated early for unexpectedly low event rates, but similarly found 
no evidence of oncologic benefit with increased risk of genitourinary 
toxicity and erectile dysfunction when an adjuvant therapy approach was 
used.440,441 One small randomized trial in Finland (n = 126) saw a 
significant improvement in 10-year survival for biochemical recurrence with 
the use of adjuvant therapy in patients with positive margins or 
extracapsular extension (HR, 0.26; 95% CI, 0.14â0.48; P < .001).442 
However, there was no difference in OS, and grade 3 adverse events 
were significantly higher in the adjuvant arm.
Several meta-analyses have also shown that adjuvant radiotherapy 
improved biochemical control, but not overall survival, in patients with 
adverse features and that the approach leads to a higher incidence of 
adverse events, though some noted that careful patient selection is 
warranted.437,443-445 Real world evidence supports the safety of this 
approach for most patients.446,447
The Panel notes immediate adjuvant radiation may be reasonable for 
patients with multiple adverse features, a population that was under-
represented in the above-mentioned clinical trials. True noninferiority of 
the delayed, early secondary therapy approach has not been 
demonstrated in patients with a high risk of recurrence after surgery.
Adjuvant or Early Secondary Therapy for pN1
Patients with positive lymph nodes found during or after radical 
prostatectomy have several disease management options to consider.
Immediate adjuvant treatment with EBRT with or without ADT is one 
approach for these patients based on historic data.448 However, 
retrospective data show that initial observation may be safe in some 
patients with pN1 disease at radical prostatectomy, because 28% of a 
cohort of 369 patients remained free from biochemical recurrence at 10 
years.449 Therefore, another option is monitoring with consideration of 
early secondary treatment for a detectable and rising PSA or PSA >0.1 
ng/mL. However, RADICALS-RT, GETUG-AFU 17, and TROG 
08.03/ANZUP RAVES have very few pN1 patients that were enrolled.439-441
An analysis of the National Cancer database identified 8074 patients with 
lymph node metastases after radical prostatectomy. 450 The results 
indicate that immediate adjuvant EBRT and ADT improved OS compared 
to monitoring for those who also had adverse pathologic features but not 
for those without these features. Therefore, use of early secondary 
radiotherapy can be considered for patients with pN1 disease, with careful 
patient selection and monitoring.
The addition of abiraterone to EBRT with ADT can also be considered for 
patients with positive lymph after surgery (category 2B). However, these 
patients comprised <5% of patients on the STAMPEDE trial, and caution 
is warranted with extrapolation of data from cN1 disease.394
Secondary Treatment for PSA Persistence or Biochemical 
Recurrence After Radical Prostatectomy
The timing of secondary treatment for patients whose only evidence of 
cancer after radical prostatectomy is a persistent or increasing PSA is 
influenced by PSA levels, PSADT, and the underlying comorbidities of the 
patient. PSADT should be calculated to inform nomogram use and 
counseling for patients with PSA persistence or recurrence after radical 
prostatectomy. Early secondary treatment (at a PSA of 0.1â0.2 ng/mL) is 
recommended for most patients who experience a biochemical recurrence 
after RP. However, patients who have a delayed recurrence (eg, many
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by MĂĄximo LĂłpez Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:29:25 PM. Copyright Â© 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 5.2026 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂ© (Oncology GuidelinesÂ©), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 5.2026 
Prostate Cancer
MS-35
years post-RP) with very slow PSA doubling times (ie, >24 months) can 
consider delaying secondary treatment, especially if they have limited life 
expectancy.
For those with PSA persistence or recurrence after RP without evidence of 
M1 disease, secondary treatment with EBRT is the preferred approach. 
Large retrospective cohort studies support the use of EBRT in the setting 
of biochemical recurrence, because it is associated with decreased all-
cause mortality and increased prostate cancer-specific survival.451,452
Numerous phase 3 randomized trials have evaluated the role of hormone 
therapy with secondary EBRT in this setting, as well as a harmonized 
aggregate meta-analysis.453-457 In general, these studies have 
demonstrated that 6 to 24 months of hormone therapy improved outcomes 
primarily for patients receiving late secondary RT (PSA levels >0.5 
ng/mL), but a smaller or no benefit is clear at lower PSA levels. For 
example, RTOG 9601 demonstrated an OS benefit with 2 years of 
bicalutamide with secondary EBRT, but enrolled patients primarily 
receiving late secondary EBRT.458 In contrast, the RADICALS-HD trial 
randomized patients to post-operative EBRT with or without 6 months of 
ADT and enrolled mostly adjuvant or early secondary EBRT patients. They 
showed that ADT did not improve MFS or OS. The DADSPORT meta-
analysis did not show an OS benefit for the addition of ADT in patients 
receiving early secondary RT, although an MFS and PCSS benefit were 
seen.459
Based on these data, short-term ADT is generally recommended to 
balance toxicity, QOL, and tumor control. However, long-term ADT may be 
preferred for patients receiving late secondary RT and/or those with 
multiple adverse pathologic features or lymph node involvement.
There remains no randomized data to support the addition of an ARPI to 
EBRT and ADT. However, abiraterone is an option for patients with lymph
node metastases (category 2B), based on extrapolation from 
STAMPEDE.394
Monitoring patients without evidence of M1 disease in the setting of PSA 
persistence or a rising PSA post-RP is another option, especially if the risk 
of rapid progression appears to be low based on factors such as PSA 
levels, PSADT, and results of nomograms and with consideration of 
patient age and comorbidities. It is important to note that some patients 
have detectable PSA after radical prostatectomy due to benign prostate 
tissue in the prostate fossa. They have low stable PSAs and a very low 
risk of prostate cancer progression.460,461 Serial PSA measurements can 
be helpful for stratifying patients at highest risk of progression and 
metastases.
Post-Radiation Recurrence
The 2006 Phoenix definition was revised by ASTRO and the RTOG in 
Phoenix: 1) PSA rise by 2 ng/mL or more above the nadir PSA is the 
standard definition for biochemical recurrence after EBRT with or without 
hormonal therapy; and 2) A recurrence evaluation should be considered 
when PSA has been confirmed to be increasing after radiation even if the 
rise above nadir is not yet 2 ng/mL, especially in candidates for additional 
local therapy who are young and healthy.462 Retaining a strict version of 
the ASTRO definition allows comparison with a large existing body of 
literature. Rapid increase of PSA may warrant evaluation (prostate biopsy) 
prior to meeting the Phoenix definition, especially in younger or healthier 
patients.
Workup for PSA recurrence or a positive DRE after RT for a patients with 
a life expectancy >5 years typically includes PSADT calculation, bone and 
soft tissue imaging, and consideration of a prostate/seminal vesicle 
biopsy. Biopsy is particularly useful if the staging workup does not reveal
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by MĂĄximo LĂłpez Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:29:25 PM. Copyright Â© 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 5.2026 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂ© (Oncology GuidelinesÂ©), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 5.2026 
Prostate Cancer
MS-36
metastatic disease and local recurrence is suspected. The junction of the 
seminal vesicle and prostate is a common site of recurrence.
Local radiation recurrences are most responsive to additional therapy 
when PSA levels at the time of treatment are low (<5 ng/mL). Options for 
therapy for those without evidence of distant metastases include 
monitoring with physical exam, PSA every 3 to 6 months, and imaging for 
symptoms or increasing PSA. For those deemed to be at higher risk of 
rapid progression, treatment options are ADT and local secondary therapy 
with or without ADT. For those with positive lymph nodes, abiraterone can 
be added. Treatment should be individualized based on the patient's risk 
of progression and the potential benefits and risks involved with each 
option.
Local secondary therapy options in the setting of RT recurrence include 
cryotherapy,463 HIFU,464-469 IRE470,471 (category 2B), reirradiation, and RP 
with PLND. The Panel recommends that patients receive multidisciplinary 
counseling about the risks and benefits of each of these options in the 
context of the available comparative literature on this topic.472,473 
Reirradiation options include LDR brachytherapy, HDR brachytherapy, 
and SBRT based on phase II and other prospective and retrospective 
studies.412,474-476 Radical prostatectomy after RT recurrence can result in 
long-term disease control, but is often associated with impotence and 
urinary incontinence.477 Overall, there is limited evidence for these 
approaches, and the Panel recommends that patients receiving local 
therapy for RT recurrence are treated within the context of clinical trials 
when available and/or at experienced centers.
Second Biochemical Recurrence
Patients who have been treated for recurrence after definitive therapy for 
localized prostate cancer may continue to experience a rise in PSA levels 
without developing metastases. The Panel refers to this disease setting as
second biochemical recurrence. A staging workup with imaging is 
recommended. For patients without evidence of lymph node or distant 
metastases, risk stratification is essential to determine treatment options 
and is based mainly on PSA levels and PSADT. The definition of high-risk 
second biochemical recurrence differs between clinical trials but is 
generally considered to include patients with a PSADT â€9 months and 
other adverse prognostic features.
For individuals deemed to be a low risk of rapid disease progression, 
monitoring until diagnosis of metastatic disease is preferred. ADT 
monotherapy is also an option, and PSA levels and PSADT should be 
considered when deciding whether to begin ADT.
For patients deemed to be at high risk of rapid disease progression, 
treatment intensification can be considered based on results of two trials 
as discussed below. Monitoring and ADT are equally reasonable options 
for these patients, and shared decision-making is key.
In the phase 3 EMBARK trial, 1068 patients with prostate cancer and high-
risk biochemical recurrence were randomly assigned into one of three 
groups using a 1:1:1 ratio.478 The âcombination groupâ received 
enzalutamide (160 mg/day) plus leuprolide, the âleuprolide-alone groupâ 
received placebo plus leuprolide, and the âmonotherapy groupâ received 
enzalutamide alone. Eligibility for the trial included the following high-risk 
criteria: M0 by CT, MRI, or bone scan; PSADT â€9 months; PSA â„2 ng/mL 
above nadir after RT or â„1 ng/mL after radical prostatectomy with or 
without postoperative RT; and patients were not considered a candidate 
for pelvic-directed therapy. Patients were monitored for a median follow-up 
time of 60.7 months. The trial demonstrated that the primary endpoint, 
metastasis-free survival, was significantly improved for enzalutamide plus 
leuprolide (HR for metastasis or death, 0.42; 95% CI, 0.30â0.61; P < .001) 
and for enzalutamide alone (HR, 0.63; 95% CI, 0.46â0.87; P = .005) when
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by MĂĄximo LĂłpez Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:29:25 PM. Copyright Â© 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 5.2026 Â© 2026 Oncology GuidelinesÂ© (Oncology GuidelinesÂ©), All rights reserved. Oncology GuidelinesÂź and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 5.2026 
Prostate Cancer
MS-37
compared to leuprolide alone. The most common adverse events 
associated with combination therapy and enzalutamide monotherapy were 
hot flashes and fatigue. Enzalutamide monotherapy was also significantly 
associated with gynecomastia (45% compared with 8% to 9% in the 
combination and leuprolide alone groups), nipple pain (15% compared 
with 1%â3%), and breast tenderness (14% compared with 1%). Based on 
these results, the Panel includes enzalutamide with or without leuprolide 
as options for patients with high-risk biochemical recurrence after maximal 
pelvic therapy in the absence of metastatic disease.
In the phase 3 PRESTO trial, 503 patients with biochemically recurrent 
prostate cancer were randomly assigned into one of three groups using a 
1:1:1 ratio.479 The three arms of the study included: ADT alone (control 
arm A), ADT plus apalutamide (experimental arm B), or ADT plus 
apalutamide plus abiraterone acetate/prednisone (AAP; experimental arm 
C). The dose of apalutamide was 240mg/day. To be eligible for the trial, 
patients had prostate cancer with biochemical recurrence after radical 
prostatectomy and the following high-risk criteria: PSADT â€9 months; PSA 
â„0.5 ng/mL; and prior adjuvant or secondary RT or not considered a 
candidate for RT. The planned duration of treatment was 52 weeks. The 
primary endpoint of PSA progression-free survival (PSA-PFS) was met in 
patients in both experimental arms. PSA progression was defined as 
serum PSA >0.2 ng/mL after completion of treatment. At the first interim 
analysis (median follow-up of 21.3 and 21.5 months in arms B and C, 
respectively), both experimental arms had significant prolongation of PSA-
PFS compared to the control arm (ADT plus apalutamide, 24.9 months; 
ADT plus apalutamide plus AAP, 26.0 months; ADT alone, 20.3 months). 
For the comparison of ADT with and without apalutamide, the HR was 
0.52 (95% CI, 0.35â0.77; P = .00047), and for the comparison of ADT plus 
apalutamide/AAP the HR was 0.48 (95% CI, 0.32â0.71; P = .00008). 
There was no difference in median time to testosterone recovery across 
treatment arms. The most prevalent treatment-associated adverse events
across treatment arms were hot flashes (78%), fatigue (55%), injection site 
reaction (33%), and hypertension (31%). Hypertension was the most 
common grade â„3 adverse event, experienced in 7%, 8%, and 19% of 
patients in arms A, B, and C, respectively. Even though the study was not 
designed to directly compare the two experimental arms, there was not 
shown to be further clinical benefit with the addition of AAP to apalutamide 
with ADT. Based on the results of this study, the Panel includes 
apalutamide plus ADT as a category 2B option for patients with non-
metastatic disease and high-risk of biochemical recurrence after radical 
prostatectomy who have received maximal pelvic therapy.
Androgen Deprivation Therapy
ADT is administered as primary systemic therapy for regional or advanced 
disease and as neoadjuvant/concomitant/adjuvant therapy in combination 
with radiation in patients with unfavorable-intermediate or higher risk 
localized prostate cancers. It is also used as palliative therapy for 
symptomatic patients with a life expectancy â€5 years.
Placing patients with early prostate cancer on ADT should not be routine 
practice.480,481 Bicalutamide monotherapy is not recommended to be used 
and is recommended when used to be in combination with ADT as per the 
FDA label.
Medical castration (ie, LHRH agonist or antagonist) and surgical castration 
(ie, bilateral orchiectomy) are equally effective options for ADT. Castrate 
levels of serum testosterone (<50 ng/dL; <1.7 nmol/L) should be achieved, 
because low nadir serum testosterone levels were shown to be associated 
with improved cause-specific survival in the PR-7 study.482 Patients who 
do not achieve adequate suppression of serum testosterone (<50 ng/dL) 
with medical or surgical castration can be considered for additional 
hormonal manipulations (with antiandrogens, LHRH antagonists, or 
steroids), although the clinical benefit remains uncertain. Monitoring
